items.body_text:
[Jorge Quintero]
What I'm trying to get my head around apt cloud is this, I understand how useful it will be for customers with products in multiple LOBs and I'm all for it, but how is that related to what we really need which is to increase revenue. Like VMWARE customers starting to leave because we stop promoting it and then the Broadcom situation, not necessarily because they did not have a portal to manage their virtual. However, this is a difficult argument since we don't have solid data. A conversation for another time. It is interesting what the board members are thinking about it.

---

[Adam Nelson]
for an apt cloud narrative

---

[Adam Nelson]
All this to put together like 2 bullets in a slide deck

---

[Adam Nelson]
haha here...

 

Here's the situation — the previous Claude thread was working from a different data source (a VMware spreadsheet with 49 client IDs) and comparing it against a dimServices snapshot. You've now given me the authoritative MRCTrend two-snapshot approach, which is what Jorge uses. The numbers are significantly different and several of the skeleton's claims are wrong. Let me lay it out.

The corrected headline (MRCTrend, Online services only):

 
Jan 31, 2024
Feb 28, 2026
Delta

Hosting
$961,862
$670,003
-$291,860 (-30.3%)

Cloud
$8,901
$9,494
+$593 (+6.7%)

Total
$970,763
$679,496
-$291,267 (-30.0%)

That's 54 unique clients in Jan 2024, 45 in Feb 2026. Annual impact: ~$3.5M.

Including pending (Provision + Migration + Upgrade/Downgrade): Feb 2026 is $796,416, narrowing the gap to -$174K (-18%).

Customer breakdown — the skeleton was wrong on almost every bucket:

Category
Skeleton said
Actual

Fully gone
10 clients, $195K
12 clients, $244K

Shrunk
15 clients, -$231K
13 clients, -$131K

Grew
24 clients, +$121K
29 clients, +$84K

The biggest factual error: WeirFoulds LLP was listed as "fully gone" at $41K. They actually grew from $37K to $44K (+$7K, +19%). That's a customer the skeleton had in the churn column who's actually expanding.

The BCOM 2000 "Rocky Linux migration" story is wrong. BCOM still has their ESXi 7.0 vHost online ($2.6K), still has RHEL guest VMs, and added another RHEL VM. There's no Rocky Linux 9 in their Feb 2026 snapshot. They grew from $2.9K to $4.4K. Good story, just not the VMware-exit story the skeleton tells.

What IS real and very compelling for the board:

The vHost-specific picture: $406K/mo → $258K/mo (-$148K, -36%). 20 clients lost all vHosts. Of those 20, only 12 left Aptum entirely — the other 8 dropped vHosts but are still spending $62K/mo on other services. That's the multi-product stickiness argument in real data.

The generation cliff is the actionable story:

Pre-5.0 hardware: 7 services, 2 months avg contract remaining — dying
Gen 5.0: 46 services, $142K/mo, 6 months avg remaining — this is the migration pipeline
Gen 6.0+: 27 services, $82K/mo, 26 months avg — healthy, recently signed

41 ESXi services across 19 clients ($124K/mo) have ≤6 months on contract. That's half the active vHost base about to come up for renewal. Each one is a migrate-or-lose decision.

The real migrations in flight (these are the proof points for the board):

Heilind: $41K online + $38K pending — upgrading ESXi 7.0 → ESXi 8.0 on new Dell 650xs hardware
Blue Yonder: $19K online + $36K pending — moving to Pro Series 6.0 with ESXi 8.0 (came back after losing all vHosts)
HYTECK: $14K online + $20K pending — interesting one: $9.2K of pending is Google Cloud. They're going multi-cloud.
Chicken Farmers: $24K online + $13K pending — vHost migration in progress

Want me to rewrite Section 2 of the skeleton with the corrected numbers from this MRCTrend data? The structure is good, the story is right (VMware base is contracting, multi-product customers are sticky, single-product ones churn), but the specific numbers and customer callouts need fixing throughout.

---

[Jorge Quintero]
yes

---

[Adam Nelson]
do you have access to this? https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1bTGiM9FparSNYgJKjd4QjTRfF1ZxR6IDQgai9R5F4HA/edit?gid=166975…

k

i'll call you in a sec

Want to hop on a call quick?

items.body_text:
[Perkins, Ciara]
Hi Erik,

That is correct, we will need the spreadsheet complete for each Payer Account that will be transferring over, as they each require a CTA.

The other option would be to transfer the billing relationship of the Payer accounts via the newer Billing Transfer feature. There are pros and cons to this feature, for example it is a simpler process, but customer's will lose cost and usage historical data, which is sometimes a deciding factor.

If you have any questions about the two options, please let me know and we can iron out all the details proactively.

Thank you,

Ciara Perkins
Multi-Cloud Business Development Representative III
(480) 936-5447

Sent: Thursday, March 26, 2026 4:38 PM
To: Matthew Carter <Matthew.Carter@aptum.com>; Nedim Deovic <Nedim.Deovic@aptum.com>
Cc: Erik Tanguay <Erik.Tanguay@aptum.com>; Sarah Blanchard <Sarah.Blanchard@aptum.com>; Marc Pare <Marc.Pare@aptum.com>; Adam Nelson <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>
Subject: Re: APTUM/AWS CTA transfer
 

Some people who received this message don't often get email from ciara.perkins@tdsynnex.com. Learn why this is important
Hi Matt,
Thank you for your time today. I wanted to recap our discussion and outline the next steps to support an April timeline for the AWS Account and DSA transfer.
AWS Partner Resell Discount (Effective April 1)
AWS partners in SPP or working with a distributor will receive a base 8% resell discount.
The discount is applied to your TD SYNNEX invoice, and you can create custom pricebooks per customer in StreamOne Ion to pass through margin as desired.

Eligible services: See Solution+Provider+Eligible+Services+List.pdf.
Services not on this list are not eligible for the 8% discount.
Exclusions:
Private Pricing Agreements (PPA)

AWS Marketplace purchases (see below)
AWS Existing Accounts (see below)
AWS Existing Accounts
Defined as any single member account with $50K+ MRR.

These accounts receive a 2% partner resell discount instead of 8%.
How TD SYNNEX Can Help:
Accounts can be transferred as-is, then workloads moved into a net-new member account after a full calendar month, removing the “Existing Account” designation. If customer needs assistance in this lift & shift migration, Aptum and/or TD SYNNEX Professional Services can assist (for a fee). Once it's transacting as a new account, the 8% base discount is enabled.
AWS Marketplace

Marketplace purchases appear as a pass-through on your's & the customer's invoice (no margin).
TD SYNNEX option: We can facilitate Marketplace Private Offers (CPPO or DSOR) with select ISVs, allowing for margin on those deals.
Margin varies by vendor, so please share a list of current or planned Marketplace vendors to align in advance.
Action Items & Next Steps

Complete TD SYNNEX Credit Application
Apply under Aptum’s US entity: Become a Customer – TD SYNNEX USA
Select Net Terms and provide financials when prompted.
Once approved, our new accounts team will confirm.

DSA Transfer
I’ve notified the AWS Distribution Program team of the planned April transfer to TD SYNNEX.
I’ll follow up on Monday, March 30 to confirm timing.
No action required from Aptum at this time.

Account Transfer
Once credit approval is complete and AWS confirms the DSA transfer, Aptum will need to notify your current distributor of the intent to transfer AWS business in April (they typically require an offboarding form).
TD SYNNEX will then initiate the CTA (Consent to Assign) process.

After the CTA form (attached) is completed by Aptum and returned to me, we’ll coordinate signatures and schedule a transfer call with your current distributor.
Deadline: All steps must be completed by April 24.
Please let me know if you have any questions. I’m also happy to set up a weekly check-in to keep everything on track—just let me know what works best for your team.
Thank you,

Ciara Perkins
Multi-Cloud Business Development Representative III
(480) 936-5447

Reserve Time on My Calendar

Meeting Summary
 
AWS Partner Program Changes (Effective April 1st)
The meeting focused on major AWS partner program changes being rolled out, with Gina (TD SYNNEX subject matter expert) presenting a comprehensive overview. Key changes include a new base discount structure, deprecation of several legacy incentives, and new operational requirements. The changes represent a significant overhaul of how AWS incentivizes channel partners, with many updates piling up simultaneously affecting TD SYNNEX's ~4,900 global partners.
Timeline of Changes:
- January 1st: PGI started; PGD and other incentives deprecated
- February 1st: All RPPA changes went into effect
- April 1st: New base benefit, deprecation of technology component incentive, new NCI, deprecation of POD, PSD, and CEI incentives
 
Partner-Based Discount (Primary Focus)
The new 8% flat front-end discount replaces the previous 3% base + 4% technical capability discount structure. This discount applies to non-PPA, non-internal spend, excluding existing accounts and marketplace revenue. TD SYNNEX passes the full discount through to partners — they do not retain any portion.
Status Details:
- New Base Discount: 8% (up from 3% base; previously 3% + 4% technical competency = 7%)
- Existing Accounts (formerly "Shareshift"): Only 2% discount applies to any member account spending >$50,000/month prior to linking
- Existing Account Policy: Currently permanent (no time limit); TD SYNNEX has lobbied AWS to reduce to 1–2 years but AWS has not agreed
- First billing reflection: May bill (for April usage) will be the first to reflect the new 8% discount
- Competency benefits: Financial discount for competency is now absorbed into base rate, but competencies still provide lead generation and increased MDF access
 
Existing Accounts / Shareshift Classification
The $50,000 threshold applies at the individual member account level, not the customer or payer account as a whole. Strategies to address existing account classification were discussed.
Status Details:
- Accounts under $49,999/month at time of linking receive full channel discount
- Accounts at $50,000+ receive only 2% (existing account rate)
- Exception process available (business use case submission), but Gina noted ~80% failure rate
- Acceptable exception reasons: prior reseller went out of business, unique services the new reseller provides that the old one cannot
- Workarounds discussed:
- Modernization into new member accounts (acceptable)
- Pure lift-and-shift to new member account (frowned upon)
- Normalizing spend across member accounts before linking
- Specific Optum fintech customer noted: Has been consuming direct; team to explore business case exception with AWS to classify as base account given Optum's involvement since inception
 
DSA Migration & Timing (Moving to TD SYNNEX)
Optum is planning to move their US legal entity first, with Canada and UK as a secondary phase. The migration process and timing requirements were clarified.
Status Details:
- Process: Submit APN ID + DSA move request by region to AWS; Rupesh to provide email address
- Deadline: DSA move and CTA must be completed before end-user reporting closes (last Friday of the month — approximately March 22–23rd for April 1st effective date)
- Consent to Assign (CTA): TD SYNNEX will prepare a consolidated document with all payer IDs; Optum signs once; then a 10–15 minute call with both distributors' operations teams to transfer root access
- Important: If DSA is not moved in time, neither old nor new distributor receives the discount (street price applies)
- Canada: Rupesh remains account manager for both US and Canada regions; billing currency can be USD or CAD based on preference
- Former aggregator behavior: May attempt to retain the partner; TD SYNNEX noted competitors have dragged their feet in the past
 
New Customer Incentive (NCI)
NCI is a new program replacing POD (Partner Originated Discount), offering an additional 4% discount for 3 years on qualifying new accounts.
Status Details:
- Eligibility: Customer must have spent less than $50,000 in the prior 12 months (~$5,000/month) before linking
- Benefit: Extra 4% discount for 3 years (vs. POD's 5% for 2 years)
- Requirement: Customer must be enrolled in Billing Transfer
- Billing Transfer overview:
- Customer retains root access
- Handshake model (similar to Microsoft Azure CSP)
- Customer cannot see partner or distributor discounts
- Removes consent-to-assign requirement
- Reduces operational complexity and customer hesitation around giving up root
 
Bad Debt / Bankruptcy Protection
Optum raised concerns about customer bankruptcies and recovery options.
Status Details:
- Billing Transfer advantage: Partner can sever the handshake, stopping future liability; AWS holds remaining spend
- Infrastructure as a service is classified as a utility globally — cannot be shut off during bankruptcy protection
- TD SYNNEX process: Documents all spend, identifies legal counsel, petitions AWS for reimbursement on behalf of partners
- TD SYNNEX pays AWS every two weeks regardless of partner/customer payment status
- TD SYNNEX has successfully petitioned AWS to absorb losses in past cases
- TD SYNNEX noted as better positioned to absorb bad debt than individual partners
 
MDF (Market Development Funds)
TD SYNNEX offers an MDF match program tied to AWS MDF allocations.
Status Details:
- AWS MDF: Advanced partners with competency can access up to $50,000/year (typically ~$25,000); AWS covers 50% after proof of performance
- TD SYNNEX Match: Matches what AWS contributes (covering the other 50%)
- Allocation: Annual basis, not quarterly/monthly; based on qualification level and partner activity
- One-off events: Partners can request ad hoc MDF participation with a business case (ROI, cost, timing)
- TD SYNNEX distributes limited AWS funding across ~1,300–1,400 North America partners; more active partners get more access
 
Payment Terms
Payment terms are determined individually based on credit rating and financials.
Status Details:
- Process: TD SYNNEX credit team reviews financials during onboarding to establish credit line and terms
- Typical terms: Net 30 standard; Net 45 likely achievable; Net 90 rare (requires ~$2–4M/month in AWS spend)
- Terms cannot be confirmed in advance — financial review required first
- Christine to follow up offline on credit/payment term specifics

DSA Migration Scope: US legal entity first; Canada and UK as secondary phase

Credit terms and onboarding would be entity-specific either way, and Ciara already has us pointed at the US credit application, so we're already down that path.

Based on all that, I agree we should go USA. The only reason I'd see to set up Canada is for future Canadian customer business, and Christine already suggested handling that as a separate track anyway.

Hi Matthew,
 
Please find the minutes from today’s call below.
 
***These are AI-generated minutes based on the discussion. Please review for accuracy.
 
Meeting Summary
 
AWS Partner Program Changes (Effective April 1st)
The meeting focused on major AWS partner program changes being rolled out, with Gina (TD SYNNEX subject matter expert) presenting a comprehensive overview. Key changes include a new base discount structure, deprecation of several legacy incentives, and new operational requirements. The changes represent a significant overhaul of how AWS incentivizes channel partners, with many updates piling up simultaneously affecting TD SYNNEX's ~4,900 global partners.
Timeline of Changes:
- January 1st: PGI started; PGD and other incentives deprecated
- February 1st: All RPPA changes went into effect
- April 1st: New base benefit, deprecation of technology component incentive, new NCI, deprecation of POD, PSD, and CEI incentives
 
Partner-Based Discount (Primary Focus)
The new 8% flat front-end discount replaces the previous 3% base + 4% technical capability discount structure. This discount applies to non-PPA, non-internal spend, excluding existing accounts and marketplace revenue. TD SYNNEX passes the full discount through to partners — they do not retain any portion.
Status Details:
- New Base Discount: 8% (up from 3% base; previously 3% + 4% technical competency = 7%)
- Existing Accounts (formerly "Shareshift"): Only 2% discount applies to any member account spending >$50,000/month prior to linking
- Existing Account Policy: Currently permanent (no time limit); TD SYNNEX has lobbied AWS to reduce to 1–2 years but AWS has not agreed
- First billing reflection: May bill (for April usage) will be the first to reflect the new 8% discount
- Competency benefits: Financial discount for competency is now absorbed into base rate, but competencies still provide lead generation and increased MDF access
 
Existing Accounts / Shareshift Classification
The $50,000 threshold applies at the individual member account level, not the customer or payer account as a whole. Strategies to address existing account classification were discussed.
Status Details:
- Accounts under $49,999/month at time of linking receive full channel discount
- Accounts at $50,000+ receive only 2% (existing account rate)
- Exception process available (business use case submission), but Gina noted ~80% failure rate
- Acceptable exception reasons: prior reseller went out of business, unique services the new reseller provides that the old one cannot
- Workarounds discussed:
- Modernization into new member accounts (acceptable)
- Pure lift-and-shift to new member account (frowned upon)
- Normalizing spend across member accounts before linking
- Specific Optum fintech customer noted: Has been consuming direct; team to explore business case exception with AWS to classify as base account given Optum's involvement since inception
 
DSA Migration & Timing (Moving to TD SYNNEX)
Optum is planning to move their US legal entity first, with Canada and UK as a secondary phase. The migration process and timing requirements were clarified.
Status Details:
- Process: Submit APN ID + DSA move request by region to AWS; Rupesh to provide email address
- Deadline: DSA move and CTA must be completed before end-user reporting closes (last Friday of the month — approximately March 22–23rd for April 1st effective date)
- Consent to Assign (CTA): TD SYNNEX will prepare a consolidated document with all payer IDs; Optum signs once; then a 10–15 minute call with both distributors' operations teams to transfer root access
- Important: If DSA is not moved in time, neither old nor new distributor receives the discount (street price applies)
- Canada: Rupesh remains account manager for both US and Canada regions; billing currency can be USD or CAD based on preference
- Former aggregator behavior: May attempt to retain the partner; TD SYNNEX noted competitors have dragged their feet in the past
 
New Customer Incentive (NCI)
NCI is a new program replacing POD (Partner Originated Discount), offering an additional 4% discount for 3 years on qualifying new accounts.
Status Details:
- Eligibility: Customer must have spent less than $50,000 in the prior 12 months (~$5,000/month) before linking
- Benefit: Extra 4% discount for 3 years (vs. POD's 5% for 2 years)
- Requirement: Customer must be enrolled in Billing Transfer
- Billing Transfer overview:
- Customer retains root access
- Handshake model (similar to Microsoft Azure CSP)
- Customer cannot see partner or distributor discounts
- Removes consent-to-assign requirement
- Reduces operational complexity and customer hesitation around giving up root
 
Bad Debt / Bankruptcy Protection
Optum raised concerns about customer bankruptcies and recovery options.
Status Details:
- Billing Transfer advantage: Partner can sever the handshake, stopping future liability; AWS holds remaining spend
- Infrastructure as a service is classified as a utility globally — cannot be shut off during bankruptcy protection
- TD SYNNEX process: Documents all spend, identifies legal counsel, petitions AWS for reimbursement on behalf of partners
- TD SYNNEX pays AWS every two weeks regardless of partner/customer payment status
- TD SYNNEX has successfully petitioned AWS to absorb losses in past cases
- TD SYNNEX noted as better positioned to absorb bad debt than individual partners
 
MDF (Market Development Funds)
TD SYNNEX offers an MDF match program tied to AWS MDF allocations.
Status Details:
- AWS MDF: Advanced partners with competency can access up to $50,000/year (typically ~$25,000); AWS covers 50% after proof of performance
- TD SYNNEX Match: Matches what AWS contributes (covering the other 50%)
- Allocation: Annual basis, not quarterly/monthly; based on qualification level and partner activity
- One-off events: Partners can request ad hoc MDF participation with a business case (ROI, cost, timing)
- TD SYNNEX distributes limited AWS funding across ~1,300–1,400 North America partners; more active partners get more access
 
Payment Terms
Payment terms are determined individually based on credit rating and financials.
Status Details:
- Process: TD SYNNEX credit team reviews financials during onboarding to establish credit line and terms
- Typical terms: Net 30 standard; Net 45 likely achievable; Net 90 rare (requires ~$2–4M/month in AWS spend)
- Terms cannot be confirmed in advance — financial review required first
- Christine to follow up offline on credit/payment term specifics
 
✅ Decisions Made
DSA Migration Scope: US legal entity first; Canada and UK as secondary phase
Billing Currency: US dollars preferred for current migration
Deck Sharing: Gina to share the comprehensive partner-facing program deck with the Optum/Matthew team

TD SYNNEX Team Structure: Rupesh = primary account manager (North America); Gina = programs SME (AWS incentives, PPA, partner-led support); Christine = escalation; Maz = supervisor/escalation; pre-sales technical resource also assigned
Gina's availability note: High demand across many partners; Rupesh coordinates SME engagement as needed

SPP certification no longer provides an incremental financial discount under the new structure
Billing Transfer is a strategic tool to reduce customer friction around root access and simplifies operational onboarding
All program changes represent a significant simultaneous shift — partners are encouraged to act quickly ahead of April 1st to avoid billing disruptions

[Adam Nelson]
Can you validate this blurb?

 
2. What's Happening to Our VMware Base Right Now

 

A year ago, our 49 VMware managed hosting customers were generating $935K/mo in total MRC (all services, not just vHosts). Today, including services currently in provision/migration, those same client IDs account for $630K/mo. That's a $306K/mo decline (-33%), or roughly $3.7M/yr in eroded revenue.
 

10 customers fully gone - $195K/mo

Centro Internacional de Maiz y Trigo: $43K/mo
WeirFoulds LLP: $41K/mo (left explicitly citing price increases)
Crealogix MBA Limited: $27K/mo
Be the brand experience: $22K/mo
Dods Group Limited: $18K/mo
StraighterLine: $15K/mo
Star One Credit Union: $11K/mo
20-20 Technologies: $10K/mo
Blue Cow Software: $4K/mo
TAB Products: $4K/mo

15 customers downsized - net $231K/mo reduction

HYTECK: $84K -> $20K (-77%). Largest single-customer decline.
Premia Solutions: $37K -> $7K (-82%)
Blue Yonder: $35K -> $19K (-45%)
Combined Metal Industries: $31K -> $17K (-45%)
Granite REIT: $26K -> $13K (-52%)
Checkout Technology: $24K -> $13K (-43%)
Adam Matthew Digital: $32K -> $24K (-24%)
Chicken Farmers of Ontario: $21K -> $14K (-31%)

24 customers grew or are growing - net +$121K/mo

Leek United Building Society: $38K -> $52K (+36%)
Townsquare Media: $103K -> $112K (+9%). They consolidated to one vHost but grew overall services across their dedicated footprint.
CITYWAY: $11K -> $17K (+59%)
Alaya Care: $7K -> $12K (+86%)
RepoSystems.Com: $7K -> $11K (+67%)
Currencies Direct: $3K -> $5K (+97%)
BCOM 2000: $3K -> $265 online today, but $10K in provision (migrating to Rocky Linux 9 on new hardware - this is a VMware-to-alternative transition happening in real time)
Cenovus Energy: $3K -> $2K online today, but $8.5K in provision (new ESXi 7.0/8.0 vHosts being built)
These are the customers who committed, expanded their relationship, or are actively investing in new infrastructure with us. This is what "sticky" looks like.
A data visibility problem worth flagging

Our Churn table captured only 15 formally cancelled services totaling $5K/mo across this same period. The actual revenue gap is $306K/mo. That means 98% of the revenue erosion is invisible to our churn tracking system. It's happening through downsizing, non-renewals, and service reductions that don't trigger a formal cancellation. If we're relying on the Churn table to understand VMware revenue health, we're seeing 2% of the picture. This is something we should fix regardless of platform direction.
What's still exposed

22 vHost services across 11 customers are still on ESXi 7.0 (end-of-support since Oct 2025). They have to upgrade or leave. CITYWAY alone is $14K/mo on 7.0.
35 of 80 active vHost services expire within 1 month. Half the remaining VMware base is month-to-month right now.
Only 30 of 80 vHost services have more than 12 months on contract.
Current VMware vHost revenue: $241K/mo across 32 customers, 80 services, 7 data centers (Toronto, Portsmouth, Atlanta, Herndon, Los Angeles, Miami, London).
What this means

The VMware managed hosting base is contracting, and the pattern is clear: single-product customers (vHost-only, shallow relationship) are the ones leaving or shrinking. Multi-product customers (dedicated servers, networking, managed services layered on top) are growing.

This is exactly the problem Apt Cloud + Aptum IaaS is built to solve. Instead of passing through Broadcom's price increases and watching customers walk, we migrate them to CloudStack/KVM where the licensing overhead is zero, wrap managed services around it, and the multi-product relationship keeps them.

BCOM 2000 is already doing it: moving from VMware to Rocky Linux on new Aptum hardware. That's a $10K/mo customer being retained through a platform transition. This is the template.

The 41 customers still here (plus 3 with active provisioning work) are the migration pipeline. The question is how fast we can move them.

items.subject: Navisite Support Renewal
items.body_text:
[Adam Nelson]
MA & Fred, 

Can you please pass this along to your teams?

----

Quick but important update I want on everyone's radar is that we are not renewing our contract with Navisite (now part of Accenture) when it expires in April.

The short of it is that the quality of their work has been declining for some time. The primary techincal folks have been leaving and being replaced with less skilled resources and most of the time it's our customers who have to deal with the consequences.

Our Service Desk team has already been handling most database work over the past few months (with fewer issues than when Navisite was involved). We've also added a contractor (Donald) which many of you have already met, who brings deeper expertise than we've ever had access to. Additionally, we're cross-training internally so we can eliminate single points of failure.

I know some of you have been hesitant to position database-related work because of past support issues. We now own this capability holistically and we've been operating for a few months already.

Data is becoming the center of many customer opportunities. I want to make sure that we are in a position of strength when it comes to those conversations and own the outcomes vs. relying on a 3rd party. 

Thank you,

Adam Nelson
Vice President, Operations
+1 (438) 802-9796
aptum.com | LinkedIn

Les inscriptions pour Fortinet TechExpo26 sont ouvertes! Retrouvez-nous le 5 mai au Grand Quai du Port de Montréal pour une journée stratégique consacrée aux défis, aux tendances et aux innovations qui redéfinissent la sécurité numérique.
Fortinet TechExpo26, c’est plus qu’un événement — c’est un point de rencontre pour les décideurs, experts TI et partenaires qui souhaitent anticiper les menaces, optimiser leurs environnements et accélérer leur transformation numérique.

• Des conférences stratégiques axées sur l’évolution du paysage des menaces.
• Des sessions spécialisées pour approfondir vos connaissances techniques.
• Des démonstrations concrètes de solutions intégrées et performantes.
• Des échanges privilégiés avec des experts Fortinet et vos pairs.

Et bien sûr, la participation est entièrement gratuite.

Grand Quai du Port de Montréal
200, rue de la Commune Ouest, Montreal

En savoir plus

Fortinet TechExpo26 is more than an event — it’s where IT leaders, security professionals, and partners come together to anticipate threats, optimize infrastructures, and accelerate digital transformation.

WHAT'S IN STORE FOR YOU:

• Strategic keynotes on the evolving threat landscape
• Specialized breakout sessions for deeper technical insights
• Live demonstrations of powerful, integrated solutions
• Meaningful networking with Fortinet experts and industry peers

And as always, attendance is completely free.

Tuesday, May 5th | 8 AM - 5:30 PM
Grand Quai du Port de Montréal
200 De la Commune St W, Montreal

From: Carlton Currey

To: Jason Auer; Charles Rutledge; David Alfaro; David White

Subject: RE: Ticket 12028 - Smart Scale - SQL Server License Verification

 

Gents,

 

None of the information provided by the customer is proof of SA. The license transfer doc even calls out that SA is not transferred. The MS Store image of 2 SQL licenses is only a indication they have some SQL license that they can use locally and not in a hosted environment. Charles has provided all the forms and links to have the customer acquire SA so that they can provide to us. I have to side with Charles on this one. I know this will cost us a customer but to be honest this is what is missing from all customer that want to BYOL. If they have SA then yeah BYOL all day, no proof of SA or retail license cannot be deployed in our hosted environment.

items.subject: Re: Smart - Scale - Ticket 12028 - Smart Scale - SQL Server License Verification
items.body_text:
[Andy Petterson]
Hi All,

I know that we've settled on telling the customer that SA is required as per our terms and conditions because of the admin overhead and risk, but I finally got an answer back from Samar at Insight confirming SA is not required for this customer by Microsoft. This is because the hardware is dedicated to the customer, and the server is physical. See screenshot below and attached thread if interested.

On the customer call they also said that they had a Microsoft audit that looked at their SQL setup and confirmed they had the appropriate licenses and have added this to a Cherwell ticket. I have not verified this on the ticket but can do if needed.

Regards,

Andy Petterson
Solutions Architect
+442380926091
aptum.com | LinkedIn

From: Adam Nelson <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>
Sent: 19 March 2026 14:00
To: Charles Rutledge <Charles.Rutledge@aptum.com>; Andy Petterson <Andy.Petterson@aptum.com>; Barbara Krzywoszanski <Barbara.Krzywoszanski@aptum.com>; Frederic Gingras <Frederic.Gingras@aptum.com>; Elizabeth Perez <Elizabeth.Perez@aptum.com>; Lacie Allen-Morley <Lacie.Allen-Morley@aptum.com>; Carlton Currey <Carlton.Currey@aptum.com>
Cc: Fred Taylor-Young <Fred.Taylor-Young@aptum.com>
Subject: Re: Smart - Scale - Ticket 12028 - Smart Scale - SQL Server License Verification 
 

Great work Andy and Charles, @Carlton Currey can you work with Andy to get a ticket raised with Microsoft to confirm please?

Thanks all

Adam Nelson
Vice President, Operation
+1 (438) 802-9796
aptum.com | LinkedIn

From: Charles Rutledge <Charles.Rutledge@aptum.com>
Sent: March 19, 2026 08:57
To: Andy Petterson <Andy.Petterson@aptum.com>; Adam Nelson <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>; Barbara Krzywoszanski <Barbara.Krzywoszanski@aptum.com>; Frederic Gingras <Frederic.Gingras@aptum.com>; Elizabeth Perez <Elizabeth.Perez@aptum.com>; Lacie Allen-Morley <Lacie.Allen-Morley@aptum.com>; Carlton Currey <Carlton.Currey@aptum.com>
Cc: Fred Taylor-Young <Fred.Taylor-Young@aptum.com>
Subject: RE: Smart - Scale - Ticket 12028 - Smart Scale - SQL Server License Verification 
 

The licensing problem involves the product key used to install SQL Server.  Microsoft gives the exact same product key to all Software Assurance (Enterprise)/MSDN (Developer)/SPLA (Hosting) customers.  Only retail product keys are unique.  Thus, in most cases, the product key is not proof of licensing.  In past audits, if Microsoft found this product key used for SQL Server, it assumed we provided the product key and thus licensing.  If we weren’t sending Microsoft their royalty for the license, we owed back licensing fees.
 
SQL Server + CAL is a retail-only product.  It is, to my knowledge, not offered under Software Assurance, nor can it offered under SPLA.
 
If a customer buys their own SQL Server licenses through Software Assurance, they have the ability to use these licenses in Azure, other public cloud, or a hosting provider.  This is done through License Mobility and requires the license holder to submit a form attesting that they have the required license.
 
Microsoft licensing is complex and my understanding is not complete.  However, since it carries a real financial liability if we get it wrong, I believe it’s important that we understand what Microsoft allows and requires.  I’ve documents that I’ve used to make sense of this.
 

Charles Rutledge

System Specialist
+1 (678) 365-2811
aptum.com | LinkedIn

From: Andy Petterson <Andy.Petterson@aptum.com> 
Sent: Thursday, March 19, 2026 8:27 AM
To: Adam Nelson <Adam.Nelson@aptum.com>; Barbara Krzywoszanski <Barbara.Krzywoszanski@aptum.com>; Frederic Gingras <Frederic.Gingras@aptum.com>; Elizabeth Perez <Elizabeth.Perez@aptum.com>; Lacie Allen-Morley <Lacie.Allen-Morley@aptum.com>; Charles Rutledge <Charles.Rutledge@aptum.com>; Carlton Currey <Carlton.Currey@aptum.com>
Cc: Fred Taylor-Young <Fred.Taylor-Young@aptum.com>
Subject: Re: Smart - Scale - Ticket 12028 - Smart Scale - SQL Server License Verification
 

Hi All,

 

Just going through the thread, checking the ticket and adding Carlton to see if he has an opinion.

 

Firstly, I don't think we are in trouble here - I think we are only responsible if we supplied them with SPLA SQL licensing and we've used the wrong number of cores or incorrect edition etc. There are no SPLA licenses on their server in Ocean, so I don't think Microsoft would even check it on an audit. If they found SQL on it we could say that the customer is using BYOL and is responsible for their own licensing (we have tickets as proof we've asked), and then they would have to produce their licenses to MS and be fined if they aren't covered correctly. @Carlton, have I got that correct?

 

Secondly, my understanding is that Charles may be incorrect (apologies Charles!), not sure if there was a recent change with the licensing models. My understanding is if this was a virtual server that can be moved between hosts, or is on a shared stack, or on our/their private cloud, or a newer version of SQL than what they are licensed for, the customer would 100% need SA on their licenses and would have to use the per core model based on the cores in the physical hosts. With Azure Arc or CSP licensing, you can now get away with just licensing the cores in the VM.

 

However, this is physical hardware dedicated to them with a Windows OS directly on top with presumably a fixed number of SQL connections, so they are allowed to use the Server license + CAL model, unless I'm missing something. @Charles was that information directly from an MS ticket? If not, we should probably open one to confirm if that model is allowed on a dedicated physical server in our data centers - I think it is now allowed.

 

In answer to the other questions, If their current licensing is confirmed as inadequate, when I compared the numbers SQL Arc would probably offer the cheapest SQL licensing for them as they can pay by the hour on their Azure consumption. If they want fixed costs, we can sell them CSP 1 year or 3 year options but the 1 year would be more expensive than Arc and the 3 year is about the same, so long as MS don't jack up their SQL PAYG pricing.

 

Regards,

 

Andy Petterson

Subject: Re: Smart - Scale - Ticket 12028 - Smart Scale - SQL Server License Verification 

 

All,

 

I want to close the loop on this. This ticket has been open for nearly a year, it's been passed around between multiple people (several of whom are no longer here). 

 

A few things I want to clarify up front:

 

This is not an Abuse issue. Abuse handles misuse of our services illegal activity like bitcoin mining. This is a commercial licensing gap. Fred TY, your team can stand down on this one. That said, we do need to clearly define what routes to Abuse vs. what's a commercial matter so we don't repeat this.

Someone needs to own this. @Elizabeth Perez since you're already in communication with the customer, can you take the lead? Frederic and Barbara, please support as needed from the commercial and SDM side.

The economics are what they are. Smart Scale pays us roughly $2,500/month. The compliant licensing would cost them ~$7,900/month on top of that. They're not going to pay that. Threatening suspension hasn't worked for a year and it's not going to start working now.

 

We are currently liable and at risk as an organization if we do nothing!

 

Here's what I think we should do:

 

@Elizabeth Perez, schedule a call with Mario (let's stop the back and forth email chains). He clearly doesn't understand (or doesn't want to understand) the issue, and a year of back-and-forth hasn't helped. I feel like we should present the following options:

They can purchase per-core licenses through us (the expensive path, don't lead with it, but it is an option)

They can procure Software Assurance directly from Microsoft and give us the License Mobility form (somebody to confirm... @Andy Petterson?)

We explore whether migrating their SQL workloads to Azure SQL makes sense, licensing is bundled in, the mobility problem goes away, and we potentially keep the relationship

Frederic flagged the Azure idea and I think it's worth exploring. @Andy Petterson, is it possible to get a cost comparison between the two?

If none of the above work, we agree on a timeline for them to move off our platform in an orderly way

 

Whatever comes out of the call, put the agreed path and deadline in writing. If they don't act, we follow through. No more open-ended limbo.

 

Let's get this resolved in the next two weeks. If anyone has questions or sees a gap in this, let me know.

 

Adam Nelson

His Abuse responsibilities were given to InfoSec to handle as best we can, and since then, we have been trying to document some of that knowledge based on our interpretation of what Abuse should handle, which does align with what Adam said: the mis-use of our services.

 

I don't believe license (mis-)use should fall under the purview of Abuse. If a customer has installed or is using software and violating the license agreement, should Aptum be policing that at all?
